Second-Order Inhomogeneous Boundary Value Problems
Introduction
Using the Criteria if the Solution of the Homogeneous BVP Is Known
Classifying Solutions of Inhomogeneous BVPs
Solving Solvable Inhomogeneous BVPs With Infinitely Many Solutions
Solving Solvable Inhomogeneous BVPs With a Unique Solutions
Prerequisites
Second-Order Homogeneous Boundary Value Problems
Introduction
?

When solving an inhomogeneous BVP, it is important to know whether the boundary conditions determine a unique solution. In this topic, we will learn how the solution behavior of an inhomogeneous BVP depends on the corresponding homogeneous BVP.

Suppose we have the second-order inhomogeneous boundary value problem (BVP) with the Robin (mixed) boundary conditions

Let the associated homogeneous BVP be with the boundary conditions

Then we have the following:

The inhomogeneous BVP has a unique solution if and only if the associated homogeneous BVP has only the trivial solution

We interpret this as follows:

  • If the homogeneous BVP has only the trivial solution then the inhomogeneous BVP has a unique solution.

  • If the homogeneous BVP has (infinitely many) nontrivial solutions, then the inhomogeneous BVP either has infinitely many solutions or no solution.

In particular, if the inhomogeneous BVP has one solution and the homogeneous BVP has a nontrivial solution then is a solution for every Therefore, the inhomogeneous BVP has infinitely many solutions.

In the next slide, we'll show how to use this criterion if the general solution of the homogeneous BVP is known.

Using the Criteria if the Solution of the Homogeneous BVP Is Known
?

Suppose the second-order homogeneous BVP

with the homogeneous boundary conditions

has the fundamental solutions and Then, the general solution is given by

Substituting this into the boundary conditions yields a linear system for the constants with coefficient matrix :

As we know, the homogeneous BVP has a unique, trivial solution if and only if

Consequently, our inhomogeneous BVP

with the Robin boundary conditions

has a unique solution if and only

We summarize as follows:

  • If then the inhomogeneous BVP has a unique solution.

  • If then the inhomogeneous BVP either has infinitely many solutions or no solution.

We'll see how this works with concrete examples on the next slides.

EXPLANATION

Suppose we have the second-order inhomogeneous boundary value problem (BVP)

with the Robin (mixed) boundary conditions

where and are continuous on and and are real constants such that

The inhomogeneous BVP has at most one solution if and only if the associated homogeneous BVP has only the trivial solution Specifically:

  • If the homogeneous BVP has the unique solution then the inhomogeneous problem either has no solution or a unique solution.

  • If the homogeneous BVP has (infinitely many) nontrivial solutions, then the inhomogeneous problem either has infinitely many solutions or no solution.

To classify the solutions of the corresponding homogeneous BVP, we define the matrix as

The homogeneous BVP has a unique, trivial solution if and only if In other words, if the determinant equals zero, then the homogeneous BVP has infinitely many solutions.

and we have

Therefore, since the corresponding homogeneous BVP has (infinitely many) nontrivial solutions, our inhomogeneous BVP

KEY PREREQUISITE
Second-Order Homogeneous Boundary Value Problems
Question 1
?
Consider the following boundary value problem (BVP).

y″−2y′−3y=5x,y(0)=2,y(1)=5,0≤x≤1

Let y1(x) and y2(x) be the fundamental solutions of the corresponding homogeneous BVP, and suppose we define the matrix A as

A=[y1(0)y2(0)y1(1)y2(1)].

Fill in the correct options in the reasoning below without calculating the solution to this BVP.

The absolute value of det(A) is
 
e3−1
cos⁡(1)
e4−1e
0
e2+e4e
.

Therefore, the corresponding homogeneous BVP
 
has no solution
has the unique solution y≡0
has infinitely many solutions
.

Therefore, our inhomogeneous BVP
 
has infinitely many solutions
has infinitely many solutions or no solution
has a unique solution
has at most one solution
.

Example: Solving Solvable Inhomogeneous BVPs With Infinitely Many Solutions
?

Consider the following boundary value problem (BVP).

The solution is given by

where is arbitrary. Find the functions and

EXPLANATION

Suppose we have the second-order inhomogeneous boundary value problem (BVP)

with the Robin (mixed) boundary conditions

where and are continuous on and and are real constants such that

If the associated homogeneous BVP has nontrivial solutions, then the inhomogeneous BVP has either no solution or infinitely many solutions.

To find the solution of an inhomogeneous boundary value problem (BVP):

  • First, we find the general solution of the corresponding homogeneous equation.

  • Then, we find a particular solution of the inhomogeneous equation.

  • Finally, we apply the boundary conditions to determine the arbitrary constants.

To find the fundamental solutions, we set The auxiliary equation is So, we have So, the fundamental solutions are and the general solution to the homogeneous equation is

Next, we seek a particular solution of the form Differentiating, we get

Substituting this into the equation, we reach (after some cancellation)

which gives and Therefore, our general solution is

Applying the condition gives which gives So, we have

Applying the condition gives which is true for all values of

Therefore, the solution to the boundary value problem is where is arbitrary.

Finally,

Note: The associated homogeneous BVP has infinitely many solutions. Therefore, the inhomogeneous BVP has either infinitely many solutions or no solution. Since we have found a solution that contains an arbitrary constant and satisfies both boundary conditions, the BVP has infinitely many solutions.

Suppose we have the second-order inhomogeneous boundary value problem (BVP)

with the Robin (mixed) boundary conditions

where and are continuous on and and are real constants such that

The inhomogeneous BVP has at most one solution if and only if the associated homogeneous BVP has only the trivial solution

To find the solution of an inhomogeneous boundary value problem (BVP):

  • First, we find the general solution of the corresponding homogeneous equation.

  • Then, we find a particular solution of the inhomogeneous equation.

  • Finally, we apply the boundary conditions to determine the arbitrary constants.

The homogeneous equation is

Note: The associated homogeneous BVP has only the trivial solution Therefore, the inhomogeneous BVP has at most one solution. Since we have found a solution satisfying both boundary conditions, the solution to this inhomogeneous BVP is unique.
